blocks.text: For the Lord honors a father above his children, and he confirms a mother's right over her children.

1–2 : God made the family hierarchical with parents above children. For the father as family head, see 7.23–26. On respect for both parents, see, e.g., Prov 1.8–9; 6.20; 20.20; 23.22; Ex 20.12; 21.15,17.
Those who honor their father atone for sins,
and those who respect their mother are like those who lay up treasure.
Those who honor their father will have joy in their own children, and when they pray they will be heard.
Those who respect their father will have long life, and those who honor their mother obey the Lord;
they will serve their parents as their masters.
3–7 : Honoring parents atones (vv. 3,14; as do almsgiving and sacrifice; see 3.30; cf. 28.1–5) and brings (Ex 20.12; Deut 5.16). It thus equals fear of the Lord (1.12).
Honor your father by word and deed, that his blessing may come upon you.
For a father's blessing strengthens the houses of the children, but a mother's curse uproots their foundations.
Do not glorify yourself by dishonoring your father, for your father's dishonor is no glory to you.
The glory of one's father is one's own glory, and it is a disgrace for children not to respect their mother.
8–11 : For the significance of a father's , see Gen 27.1–29; 48.15–20; 49.1–28. Dishonoring parents brings , that is, shame.
My child, help your father in his old age, and do not grieve him as long as he lives;
even if his mind fails, be patient with him; because you have all your faculties do not despise him.
For kindness to a father will not be forgotten, and will be credited to you against your sins;
in the day of your distress it will be remembered in your favor; like frost in fair weather, your sins will melt away.
Whoever forsakes a father is like a blasphemer, and whoever angers a mother is cursed by the Lord.
3.1–16: Duties to parents.
16 : The invoking of emphasizes that true piety is keeping God's commandments.
My child, perform your tasks with humility; then you will be loved by those whom God accepts.
The greater you are, the more you must humble yourself; so you will find favor in the sight of the Lord.
For great is the might of the Lord; but by the humble he is glorified.
17–20 : is a marker of piety (1.27) and so wins God's favor.
Neither seek what is too difficult for you, nor investigate what is beyond your power.
21 : may refer to Jewish apocalyptic or Greek philosophical ideas about the secrets of creation; cf. Prov 30.18.
Reflect upon what you have been commanded, for what is hidden is not your concern.
22 : Wisdom and knowledge come from what is (the Torah) and the (v. 29) of the sages, two foundational sources of wisdom for Ben Sira.
Do not meddle in matters that are beyond you, for more than you can understand has been shown you.
For their conceit has led many astray, and wrong opinion has impaired their judgment.
Without eyes there is no light; without knowledge there is no wisdom.
A stubborn mind will fare badly at the end, and whoever loves danger will perish in it.
A stubborn mind will be burdened by troubles, and the sinner adds sin to sins.
When calamity befalls the proud, there is no healing, for an evil plant has taken root in him.
The mind of the intelligent appreciates proverbs, and an attentive ear is the desire of the wise.
3.17–29: Humility.
As water extinguishes a blazing fire, so almsgiving atones for sin.
Those who repay favors give thought to the future; when they fall they will find support.
3.30–4.10: Almsgiving.
